speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, November 7, 1995. We're speaking with Mrs. Aranka Siegal. Mrs. Siegal, you were describing the day you were taken to the ghetto. Where was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was right in our town. And it was probably like maybe two miles from our house. I don't think it was much more. We had to pass a lumber yard on the left and a big park on the right, where I used to play prior to the ghetto. And now we were taken to the brick factory. As I said, where they had put us was in these long barracks that had just tile roofs and no walls, because they had built these to dry the bricks in. And they had, like, these little carts, and they had actual tracks for the little carts, like railroad tracks, that they would bring the bricks in and put it on shelves and leave it there to dry. But of course, by the time we got there, all this had been cleared out. And all we had was a roof over our head and no walls and lots of in the rain for six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, of course, we had to sleep and sit and everything on the earthen floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was you and your older-- Violet?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother and my sister Violet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --And myself and my little brother Sandorn and my little sister Joli. And we always refer to the two younger ones as the little ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have ample food, or did you run out of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. We didn't have ample food by far. No, we were hungry in the ghetto at all times. As a matter of fact, Mother's toast was a godsend. She would give us, you know, a piece, a couple of times a day, in between our noon meal, which they gave us one meal during the noon time, you know some kind of a soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which consisted of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a soup and whatever they had to put in it. Mostly, they had like different kind of beets and turnips and such. Because meat was not available, you know, and fats were hard to get and so forth. So it was mostly a vegetarian type of soup or stew or whatever. And that we got with a piece of bread. And there even, my mother, you know, always tried to keep the family together, even after my father left, you know. She'd set the table for the noon meal, and she'd go through this ritual of always having a pot of soup for us. Because there, too, food was very, very difficult to get. But we always had her bread. She always managed to bake her bread, because she had managed to get-- I guess, on black market-- several sacks of flour. And she had hidden it inside the woodshed under the wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was she able to make soup every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, well, a lot of it came from our vegetable garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: She brought that with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you mean in the ghetto. Oh, in the ghetto, we didn't cook. We have no facilities, no. This was all cooked for us in a communal kitchen. And then they would bring it and distribute it in the barrack. But mother had managed to bring a pot from home, hoping that she might be able to cook. And instead of letting each of us collect individual bowls of soup, she would come with her pot. And she would have them collect, you know, give us all the soup in the pot. And she would take it back to our little spot in this barrack. Because they assigned us so much room. From here to here, it's this family. From here to here, it's that family. And of course, we mingled around. But basically, that's where we dropped our little bundles, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she would bring it back there. And she brought some seasonings from home, little herbs, believe it or not. I mean, it was unbelievable to watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: She'd take out her little jars, and she'd sprinkle those things, a little salt, into the pot and stir it around. And then she served it to us, instead of each of us getting a bowl and just kind of, you know, drink it. And we would sit around in our little space. And then after a couple of days, she had managed to get a few nails from the man who was in charge of our barrack, Mr. Schuster. And she got a rock. And she took these beautiful bedspreads that were always her treasure at home-- she bought it at some Persian market, she told us. And they were very, very beautiful tapestries. And she had nailed them up on the beam of the barrack, to give us a little privacy-- one sheet and two of these bedspreads. And we had, like, this little tent, where she had a little privacy for us, where she managed to get a little water, wash our hair, or just give the little ones a little bath and that kind of thing. But it was cold, always cold. And you know, when you're hungry, you're twice as cold. You're always shivering.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other type of sanitary facilities were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the latrine that they built us was out in the open. And the guards walked back and forth. So you can picture yourself being able to use the bathroom, when there is all this going back and forth. Absolutely not hidden from anything, just out in the open. That's where we had the latrines. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, at first, we waited as long as we could to go. And we would have preferred to go in the evening, you know, when we weren't so visible. But of course, there was no such a thing, because there was the curfew. They didn't let us out past a certain hour, so we certainly couldn't go in the dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then if some of the children had to go during the night, you know, and they would go someplace outside the barrack, because you couldn't go any place else, then we had hell to catch the next day. And we were called pigs and all this sort of stuff, you know-- dirty swine and all that, because the children had to go to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was life in the ghetto. And again, the young people had gotten together, and they were helping, you know, out the older people, doing whatever they could. And they even had, like, a storehouse from the stuff that they bought in there. And they tried to do, you know, as much as they could. And they had a makeshift hospital set up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of the young boys had formed a little underground group, where they had smuggled in some ammunition. And they were going to blow up the trains, when they came to take us to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they never got to do it. Because first of all, they were told that if there was any kind of retaliation or any kind of-- whatever it's called, they are going to kill all the people in the infirmity first.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there was any--

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was, you know, any--

speaker: interviewer
text: Resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, resistance, that's the word I was looking for. Any resistance, that they would kill all the people in the infirmity first. And there would be just no reason to do it, because they have so many means in getting us there. So that if we blow up the tracks or anything like that, they'll find another way. But anyhow, the kids were really hoping and plotting that they were going to do something, in spite of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then the trains arrived, you know, when we were least expecting it, again, in the middle of the day, which is not when we were expecting it, you know? About how many weeks were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks, six weeks under these conditions in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any knowledge where the trains might be taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they told us in the last minute, just before they were taking us, not to fret because they were just taking us to Germany. And we were going to work in factories, replacing the Germans who were in the war. We were taking their place and that we would be treated well, because we would be, you know, factory workers. And my best friend Judy in the ghetto even said to me-- she came from Budapest and was a very sophisticated young woman, she went to one of these very advanced schools. And she said to me, you know, that would be kind of fun. We'll go to Germany and we'll perfect our German. And we'll be treated like adults, because we'll be working in a factory rather than we're always treated like kids here, because we were too young to join the youth group, you know. And she made it even sound like it was going to be a big adventure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judy Gerber, Judy Gerber. And Judy Gerber was one of these people who-- well, she was years ahead of me, even though she was only about six months older, because of her education and having grown up in a big city. And she was-- she came from very liberal parents and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she really-- but unfortunately, no tradition in the home or anything, which I think finished her off in a way. Because she had nothing to hold on to. I had found during my survival and looking back that the most important things that we humans have is family and tradition and religion, of course, for those who are religious is very important. But if you don't have that to hold onto, do you know? You're just absolutely lost when you're in an extreme situation. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So Judy died in Auschwitz. She died in Auschwitz. She had a lot of problems dealing with what was happening. I mean, everybody did. But hers was just worse because she was such an intellectual. And she tried to sort of explain everything. You know, everything had to be explained. And there was no way to explain what we found over there. So she couldn't deal with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll first just tell you that they took us by freight cars, as most people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the 9th of May. My sister had her birthday May 1 in the ghetto. She was 16. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her birthday?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother help celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the young men that I knew, actually, who worked for the bakery outside had bought a loaf of bread. So we all had that. And she got little gifts. Like, Judy gave her one of her books. And I wrote her a poem and things like that. So we did celebrate her birthday the best we could over there. And then they had these freight cars come. And they, as I said, tried to calm us down. They even told us to tie all our little belongings, whatever we had-- my mother's bedspread and so forth-- into bundles and put a certain address they gave us on these bundles. So there were all the women with the pencils, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Writing the addresses and all. Oh, it was a phony address. No, I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Phony address-- Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you the city? A phony city name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- everything was phony. Everything was phony, because of course they had no intentions of bringing any of our things. This was just so we wouldn't try to carry it on the freight car. We should leave everything behind, because there was no room. They put 100 people into each freight car, 100. And you know, I always had a vision of the freight car being quite large, because so many of us were in there, until I went to Washington, where they happened to have at the Holocaust Museum a freight car, an actual freight car. And when I saw it, I realized how small it was. I could not for the life of me-- I mean, I found the spot, you know, within this freight car, as compared to the freight car I was in, exactly where I was most of the time and so forth. But the car was so tiny that I just couldn't believe that 100 of us were in that car. But that's how they jammed us in, 100 to a car. And there were old and sick people, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a closed--

speaker: interviewee
text: No facilities. They locked the doors the minute they jammed the 100 in. And I mean literally jammed us in like sardines, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a closed freight car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Closed freight car with just a little tiny openings that they have for the animals, you know, for air on top. And we would climb up on each other's shoulders, the children. And we would look out because the adults kept saying, see if you can see a sign. See if you can see a station, you know, where there might be a sign, to see where we're going, you know? Because they were trying to figure out what direction we were going and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever the kids could read-- I never was on the top there. And they would yell back, you know, to the parents. And they were trying to figure it out. Like, Mr. Schuster, who was in charge of us, was in our car. So were you with your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother and sister and the two little ones. And mother was so, so, so depressed. I thought she was depressed before. But at this point, she was beyond depressed. Really, she was like-- I'm not sure that she was totally aware of what was going on. And the two little ones clung to her. And the little Joli kept wanting water. She was thirsty. She was tired. And of course, my mother couldn't help her. There was nothing to give her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your four-year-old sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My four year old sister was saying, I'm sleepy, I'm tired. And really she wasn't a child to complain. Because for years now, you know, she had it tough, because my mother was away from the house so much of the time, trying to gather food and so forth. But in the cattle car, I guess she was very frightened. And she just kept asking for food or water or something. And mother just rocked her in her lap. My little brother stayed very close to her. And I found the whole thing there so depressing that I left my mother. I somehow worked my way through the cattle car, over to the other side where there were these young people that had kept climbing up to look out the window. And I spent most of the time with them. I wasn't even near my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your older sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the time. She went back and forth between my mother and the young people. Because the young people sort of all worked their way over to one side of the cattle car, by the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to push out the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those windows were just little bitty things. And the doors were bolted. No, no, no, you couldn't get out. But they dropped little message, they did that, they dropped little messages. You know, who was in the car and so forth and where we think that they're taking us and so forth. But no, there was no way to get out. But my largest regret, looking back on that particular experience, the ride from barracks [INAUDIBLE] to Auschwitz, is that I did not spend that time with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And of course, I didn't know that was my last chance to be with her. I had no idea. So I wasn't with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- [SIGH] Was there a name? Even if it was bogus, was there a name written on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just before the train closed, one of the trainmen, the people who came to the train, said Auschwitz. And then somebody noticed that that was written on one of the cattle cars, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Oswiecim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz, Auschwitz. But maybe it was spelled that way. You know, I was such a kid. I don't remember. Maybe it was spelled, you know, the Polish way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother leaned out, actually, and asked somebody, I think it was one of the Hungarian police, do you know where Auschwitz is? But she never got the answer because they pushed her into the cart, and they closed the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sound of the bolting of the door is something else that will remain with me for the rest of my life. That metallic clank was so unbelievably penetrating that every time I hear something metallic fall on, say, cement or something and it makes that sound, I still jump. Because those doors, when they closed, it just meant, you know, the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cattle car, no, we were hot. Because it was a very warm day in May. And we were so many people pushed so close together, we were hot. One woman gave birth in the cattle car. There was not even room. She was-- you know, not enough room for her to even lie down to give birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone who was trying to have a sense of humor in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember. Us kids tried to, like, you know, say that it won't be so bad and, you know, that the parents are thinking it's going to be much worse than it will be. Once we get out of the cattle cars, you know, we'll be all right. And it's just so suffocating in there. We have no air.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were saying, you know, that it's going to be OK. But no, I don't remember any sense of humor in the cattle cars. I think even the kids my age and a little older who were trying to make light of it were scared stiff, you know? Was there a lot of weeping and crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the older people, in the beginning. Then they frankly had no strength to cry. They were just exhausted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the cattle car two days and two nights. Actually, we arrived on the second day. And they kept us in the cattle car for quite a while after we arrived. And some of the kids that were looking outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said that there were big smokestacks. So we thought, well, that's the factories, you know? And that they could smell very pungent smoke. They said, oh, pew, you know, it smells so bad. And some people said, well, maybe it's a rubber factory, you know? And different people were pushing to have a look. Did the train ever stop on route to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it stopped about three or four times maybe. And they let us empty the two buckets, which were our facilities. But, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were just handing it down-- just handed it down. Nobody got to get off the train. We just handed it down, and they handed us back the bucket with filthy words about us being dirty pigs and all this sort of stuff, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see local people in a different towns where it stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it didn't stop during the day. We stopped at night. You couldn't see anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Auschwitz, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you smelled the pungent smell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just about the last one to get off the train. I just didn't want to get off somehow. My older sister got off, and she got-- my mother handed down the two little ones to her. And then my mother got off. And my mother was yelling for me to jump. And you know, to show you what a kid I was, the cattle car is about this high. So here, I keep saying that I wasn't scared for myself. I was scared to jump. Because, you know, for the animals, they put this slanted down, to walk down. But for us, they didn't do anything. Some of the prisoners who were there from before-- like, I guess a lot of them were Polish prisoners because they got there way years before us-- they were trying to help us down. Because there were a lot of people there, you know, really old and sick. And spending that kind of time in the cattle cars, they were in pretty bad shape. So they were helping people down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did someone help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, finally, somebody had to help me down, because I didn't jump. And this man in this blue and white striped uniform came over to me, totally emaciated. Oh, he looked like an absolute living skeleton. He has a little round cap from the same cloth as his uniform was. And I remember on his shaved head, it was just riding back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, how old are you? And I said, 14. But I really wasn't 14 yet. I had my 14th birthday in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, no, no. You're not 14. You're 16, 16. He spoke in Yiddish. He said, you're 16. I said, no, my sister over there, she is 16. And he turned to her, and he said, you're 17. And then he handed me down to my mother. And of course they put us quickly into lines of five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started marching us. There was all kinds of screaming and chaos, because they were separating the families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did everything happen rapidly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- extremely rapidly, extremely rapidly. Because so much was happening that, you know, even that made it all sound-- we were being rushed, rushed, rushed. [DRINKS] Schnell, schnell, schnell is all we heard. And then we saw German soldiers on horses, horseback, riding between us, back and forth. And they were trying to beat us into the line-- five abreast, five abreast, men to the right, women to the left. And as they were being separated-- sons, mothers, fathers, brothers, you know, husbands, wives-- as they were being pulled apart, there was such crying and carrying on. It was just absolutely unbelievable to describe. And suddenly as we are in line, my mother looks real happy after all this depression. And she said, they're not going to separate us. They're going to let us be, because we're five. This made all the difference to her. But as the lines moved along-- quickly, quickly, quickly-- suddenly, as the lines were disappearing in the front, I wasn't paying attention, because I kept looking in the other lines of the men and everything. Suddenly, we are faced with these Germans in uniform. All of them in full uniform, four of them there. Except one of them, who had his white sleeves rolled up and the black britches. And he has a baton. We later found out he was Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time, Dr. Mengele didn't mean anything, you know, more to me than just a doctor, you know? I don't even know if I heard the name. But later, I found out who he was. And as we are facing him now, he puts this baton on my mother's shoulder. And he said, how old are you? And my mother said, 42, which is what she was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he puts the baton on my sister's shoulder. And she remembered what the man said. Actually, we talked about it in the line. And mother said, you do as he said, because he seems to know the rules here. So she quickly said, 17. So when he asked me, I said-- I don't even think he asked me, I think I just blurted it out-- 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, OK. And he took my mother and started to push her to the right, with my older sister and me. And my mother just stopped dead, like she was defying him. And she said, no. I have to go with the little ones, because they'll need me more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she picked up my little four-year-old sister in her arms. And she took Sandorn by the hand. And she started walking with them to the left, where he was pushing the children by themselves. And then she turned to look at us. And my sister later told me that she spoke, but I didn't hear a word. But my sister said that mother told her, look after her, meaning me. But I never heard this. All I know is I saw my mother. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And she looked so terrible, you know? She had very pretty green eyes. She was very fair, like my sister, blonde. They were almost like more mulattoes, very, very white skin. My sister is still like that, I'll show you a picture later-- very blonde, very fair, like natural platinum blondes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that white skin was gray, and her green eyes were black. And that's all I saw. I didn't hear her speak. [SOBBING] And suddenly as I was watching her, she disappeared in the crowd with the old and the sick and the crippled. And that was the last time I ever saw her or the two little ones. And then my sister and I were pushed over to the right and made to line up again. And we lined up with Mrs. Hollander, a woman from our city, and her two daughters, Magda and Elsa, who are still in touch with. All three of them survived, miraculously, I'm sure because their mother was with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selection was going on right then and there. But we didn't know that. You know, they were selecting for work. So anybody over 16 and who looked healthy was going to work. They selected the men at one area and the women at the other. And then anybody who looked old and sick they pushed to the left with the children. And if the mother looked young enough to work, they took the child from her arm, and they'd shoo it into this [SIGHS] --Red Cross, like a truck. But of course, there was no Red Cross. But they had a white truck with a red cross on it. And they took the children from their mothers' arms and they just threw them in there. And one particular mother, who I didn't know before from our city, but I was told later who she was, she just went crazy on the spot. As they took the child from her, she started ripping off her clothes and she just absolutely went crazy. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they pushed her in that area too, you know. Anybody who acted up went to the gas chambers. So some of the mothers that they wanted to work, they would take to the right and grab the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take to the right and take the children. But her, because she carried on the way she did--

speaker: interviewer
text: She went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they pushed her to the left also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you describe-- continue describing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us to a bathhouse, where they shaved our hair and our pubic hair and our underarms. They made us all take off all our clothes and told us to fold it very neatly and put it alongside the long wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who shaved-- who shaved your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners-- prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Female prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Male-- male prisoners. And they had these little clippers. And here we were, you know, brought up very modest. OK, we had the ghetto with the latrines, but still. We are all shriveled up, trying to cover as much of our body as we can. And they say, fold your clothes very neatly and put it on the benches along the wall, so when after you have a shower, you come back and get it. And your shoes under the bench. And they're giving us all these directions. Line up--

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners? Who's giving you the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's all German, all German soldiers. Now were all German soldiers there, with their rifles, with their sticks. And they're hitting anybody who is not doing what they're supposed to do. Or if you're frozen, and you kind of don't know how to move to get undressed or whatever, they woke you up with the stick. They gave you, you know-- they hit you, to kind of bring you back to yourself. So, you know, you couldn't block out if you wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the area where you had to undress, was that a closed area? Was it a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a huge white room. It looked like the whole thing was marble, all white, white, white marble benches, white marble floors. And they made us go single file here to have our hair cut. And when I saw them shaving these people, I started to cry. I think that's the first I cried. And my sister said to me, OK, I'll go first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were my sister's gorgeous blonde hair on the floor. And then they make her raise her arms so they can do her underarms and the pubic hair, all done by a man. And they're telling the man, schnell, schnell, schnell. Thank you. Thank you very much.
